was held on June 28, 2012, at [Prudential Center](/wiki/Prudential_Center "Prudential Center") in [Newark, New Jersey](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey"). The draft started at 7:00 pm [Eastern Daylight Time](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone_(North_America) "Eastern Time Zone (North America)") (2300 [UTC](/wiki/Coordinated_Universal_Time "Coordinated Universal Time")), and was broadcast in the United States on [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN"). In this [draft](/wiki/Draft_(sports) "Draft (sports)"), [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") (NBA) teams took turns selecting amateur U.S. [college basketball](/wiki/College_basketball "College basketball") players and other eligible players, including international players. The [New Orleans Hornets](/wiki/New_Orleans_Hornets "New Orleans Hornets"), who had a 13.70 percent probability of obtaining the first selection, won the [NBA draft lottery](/wiki/NBA_draft_lottery "NBA draft lottery") on May 30. The [Charlotte Bobcats](/wiki/Charlotte_Bobcats "Charlotte Bobcats") and the [Washington Wizards](/wiki/Washington_Wizards "Washington Wizards") were second and third, respectively. This draft marked the first time that the first two players selected were from the same school ([Anthony Davis](/wiki/Anthony_Davis "Anthony Davis") and [Michael Kidd-Gilchrist](/wiki/Michael_Kidd-Gilchrist "Michael Kidd-Gilchrist") were teammates at [Kentucky](/wiki/Kentucky_Wildcats_men%27s_basketball "Kentucky Wildcats men's basketball")).[[1]](#cite_note-1) It also set a record of having six players from one school (Kentucky) being selected in the two rounds of the draft and was the first draft to have the first three selections be college freshmen all from the same conference ([Southeastern Conference](/wiki/Southeastern_Conference "Southeastern Conference")). [Bernard James](/wiki/Bernard_James "Bernard James") was the oldest player drafted in an NBA draft, being 27 years old at the time of the draft.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The **2012 NBA draft** was held on June 28, 2012, at [Prudential Center](/wiki/Prudential_Center "Prudential Center") in [Newark, New Jersey](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey"). The draft started at 7:00 pm [Eastern Daylight Time](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone_(North_America) "Eastern Time Zone (North America)") (2300 [UTC](/wiki/Coordinated_Universal_Time "Coordinated Universal Time")), and was broadcast in the United States on [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN"). In this [draft](/wiki/Draft_(sports) "Draft (sports)"), [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") (NBA) teams took turns selecting amateur U.S. [college basketball](/wiki/College_basketball "College basketball") players and other eligible players, including international players. The [New Orleans Hornets](/wiki/New_Orleans_Hornets "New Orleans Hornets"), who had a 13.70 percent probability of obtaining the first selection, won the [NBA draft lottery](/wiki/NBA_draft_lottery "NBA draft lottery") on May 30. The [Charlotte Bobcats](/wiki/Charlotte_Bobcats "Charlotte Bobcats") and the [Washington Wizards](/wiki/Washington_Wizards "Washington Wizards") were second and third, respectively. This draft marked the first time that the first two players selected were from the same school ([Anthony Davis](/wiki/Anthony_Davis "Anthony Davis") and [Michael Kidd-Gilchrist](/wiki/Michael_Kidd-Gilchrist "Michael Kidd-Gilchrist") were teammates at [Kentucky](/wiki/Kentucky_Wildcats_men%27s_basketball "Kentucky Wildcats men's basketball")).[[1]](#cite_note-1) It also set a record of having six players from one school (Kentucky) being selected in the two rounds of the draft and was the first draft to have the first three selections be college freshmen all from the same conference ([Southeastern Conference](/wiki/Southeastern_Conference "Southeastern Conference")). [Bernard James](/wiki/Bernard_James "Bernard James") was the oldest player drafted in an NBA draft, being 27 years old at the time of the draft.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The draft contained seven players who combined for 10 championships as well as having Anthony Davis and [Damian Lillard](/wiki/Damian_Lillard "Damian Lillard") both named to the [NBA 75th Anniversary Team](/wiki/NBA_75th_Anniversary_Team "NBA 75th Anniversary Team") in 2021.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Six players have participated in an [All-Star Game](/wiki/NBA_All-Star_Game "NBA All-Star Game"). [Draymond Green](/wiki/Draymond_Green "Draymond Green") won [NBA championships](/wiki/NBA_championship "NBA championship") with the [Golden State Warriors](/wiki/Golden_State_Warriors "Golden State Warriors") in [2015](/wiki/2015_NBA_Finals "2015 NBA Finals"), [2017](/wiki/2017_NBA_Finals "2017 NBA Finals"), [2018](/wiki/2018_NBA_Finals "2018 NBA Finals"), and [2022](/wiki/2022_NBA_Finals "2022 NBA Finals"), as well as being named to the [All-NBA](/wiki/All-NBA_Team "All-NBA Team") second team in 2016 and the third team in 2017. Green was named the [Defensive Player of the Year](/wiki/NBA_Defensive_Player_of_the_Year_Award "NBA Defensive Player of the Year Award") in [2017](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_NBA_season "2016–17 NBA season"), while also [leading the league in steals](/wiki/List_of_National_Basketball_Association_annual_steals_leaders "List of National Basketball Association annual steals leaders") that season. He was named to the [All-Defensive](/wiki/NBA_All-Defensive_Team "NBA All-Defensive Team") first team in [2015](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_NBA_season "2014–15 NBA season")–2017, [2021](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_NBA_season "2020–21 NBA season"), and [2025](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_NBA_season "2024–25 NBA season"), and named to the second team in [2018](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_NBA_season "2017–18 NBA season")–[2019](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_NBA_season "2018–19 NBA season") and [2022](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_NBA_season "2021–22 NBA season")–[2023](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_NBA_season "2022–23 NBA season"). He was an All-Star in [2016](/wiki/2016_NBA_All-Star_Game "2016 NBA All-Star Game")–[2018](/wiki/2018_NBA_All-Star_Game "2018 NBA All-Star Game") and [2022](/wiki/2022_NBA_All-Star_Game "2022 NBA All-Star Game").[[4]](#cite_note-DraymondGreenStats-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) Anthony Davis won a championship with the [Los Angeles Lakers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Lakers "Los Angeles Lakers") in [2020](/wiki/2020_NBA_Finals "2020 NBA Finals") and was All-NBA 5 times; first team in 2015, 2017–2018, and [2020](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_NBA_season "2019–20 NBA season"), and second team in [2024](/wiki/2023%E2%80%9324_NBA_season "2023–24 NBA season"). Davis was named to the All-Defensive first team in 2018, 2020, and 2024, and second team in 2015 and 2017. He was the [NBA blocks leader](/wiki/List_of_National_Basketball_Association_annual_blocks_leaders "List of National Basketball Association annual blocks leaders") in [2014](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_NBA_season "2013–14 NBA season"), 2015, and 2018. Davis was an All-Star from [2014](/wiki/2014_NBA_All-Star_Game "2014 NBA All-Star Game")–[2021](/wiki/2021_NBA_All-Star_Game "2021 NBA All-Star Game"), and [2024](/wiki/2024_NBA_All-Star_Game "2024 NBA All-Star Game")–[2025](/wiki/2025_NBA_All-Star_Game "2025 NBA All-Star Game"), as well as the [NBA All Star Game MVP](/wiki/NBA_All_Star_Game_MVP "NBA All Star Game MVP") in [2017](/wiki/2017_NBA_All-Star_Game "2017 NBA All-Star Game").[[6]](#cite_note-AnthonyDavisStats-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) Damian Lillard became the fourth player to be unanimously named [Rookie of the Year](/wiki/NBA_Rookie_of_the_Year_Award "NBA Rookie of the Year Award").[[8]](#cite_note-8) Lillard was a member of the All-NBA first team in 2018, second team in 2016, 2019–2021, and third team in 2014, 2023. He was an All-Star in 2014, [2015](/wiki/2015_NBA_All-Star_Game "2015 NBA All-Star Game"), 2018–2021, and [2023](/wiki/2023_NBA_All-Star_Game "2023 NBA All-Star Game")–2025, the All-Star Game MVP in 2024, as well as the winner of the [Three-Point Contest](/wiki/Three-Point_Contest "Three-Point Contest") in 2023, 2024, and [2026](/wiki/2026_NBA_All-Star_Game "2026 NBA All-Star Game").[[9]](#cite_note-DamianLillardStats-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-Lillard2024AllStarAwards-11) [Khris Middleton](/wiki/Khris_Middleton "Khris Middleton") won a championship with the [Milwaukee Bucks](/wiki/Milwaukee_Bucks "Milwaukee Bucks") in [2021](/wiki/2021_NBA_Finals "2021 NBA Finals") and was selected as an All-Star in [2019](/wiki/2019_NBA_All-Star_Game "2019 NBA All-Star Game")–[2020](/wiki/2020_NBA_All-Star_Game "2020 NBA All-Star Game"), and 2022.[[12]](#cite_note-KhrisMiddletonStats-12) As of 2025, Davis, [Bradley Beal](/wiki/Bradley_Beal "Bradley Beal"), Lillard, [Harrison Barnes](/wiki/Harrison_Barnes "Harrison Barnes"), [Andre Drummond](/wiki/Andre_Drummond "Andre Drummond"), Green, and Middleton are the only players from this draft who are still playing in the NBA.

Of the players drafted, 30 were [forwards](/wiki/Forward_(basketball) "Forward (basketball)"), 21 were [guards](/wiki/Guard_(basketball) "Guard (basketball)"), and 9 were [centers](/wiki/Center_(basketball) "Center (basketball)"). The draft marked the first appearance of the [Brooklyn Nets](/wiki/Brooklyn_Nets "Brooklyn Nets") and the last draft appearance for the New Orleans Hornets. After the [2012–13 season](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_NBA_season "2012–13 NBA season"), the franchise was renamed as the [New Orleans Pelicans](/wiki/New_Orleans_Pelicans "New Orleans Pelicans"). New Orleans made their first draft appearance as the Pelicans in [2013](/wiki/2013_NBA_draft "2013 NBA draft"). Four of the players selected in this draft never played in an NBA game throughout their professional basketball careers. Two of those players were the sole selection of the draft by their respective teams: [İlkan Karaman](/wiki/%C4%B0lkan_Karaman "İlkan Karaman") (Brooklyn's only pick) and [Marcus Denmon](/wiki/Marcus_Denmon "Marcus Denmon") (San Antonio's only pick).

## Draft lottery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2012_NBA_draft&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Draft lottery")]

Further information: [NBA draft lottery](/wiki/NBA_draft_lottery "NBA draft lottery")

The first 14 picks in the draft belong to teams that missed the [playoffs](/wiki/2012_NBA_playoffs "2012 NBA playoffs"); the order was determined through a lottery. The lottery was only used to determine the teams that obtained the first three picks in the draft. The remaining first-round picks and the second-round picks were assigned to teams in reverse order of their win–loss record in the [previous season](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_NBA_season "2011–12 NBA season").[[86]](#cite_note-DraftLotteryRules-125) To determine the top three picks, fourteen ping pong balls numbered 1–14 were placed into a lottery machine. Four random balls were selected for each of the three picks and there were a total of 1,001 combinations. The lottery was weighted so that the teams with the worst records had better chances at the first pick. For example, the [Charlotte Bobcats](/wiki/Charlotte_Bobcats "Charlotte Bobcats") had the worst record from the previous season, so they were assigned 250 of the 1,001 combinations, which meant they had a 25% chance of getting the first pick.[[87]](#cite_note-DraftLotteryExplained-126)

The lottery was held on May 30, 2012, in the [Disney/ABC Times Square Studio](/wiki/Times_Square_Studios "Times Square Studios") in New York City.[[88]](#cite_note-127) The [New Orleans Hornets](/wiki/New_Orleans_Hornets "New Orleans Hornets") won the rights to the [first overall selection](/wiki/List_of_first_overall_NBA_draft_picks "List of first overall NBA draft picks") with a 13.7% chance to win. The Hornets were a league-owned team at the time, leading to continued [conspiracy theories about the lottery process](/wiki/NBA_draft_conspiracy "NBA draft conspiracy").[[89]](#cite_note-128)[[90]](#cite_note-129)[[91]](#cite_note-130)[[92]](#cite_note-131) The Bobcats won the second overall pick despite having the highest odds to win the lottery.[[93]](#cite_note-DraftLotteryResults-132)

## Eligibility rules

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2012_NBA_draft&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Eligibility rules")]

Main article: [Eligibility for the NBA draft](/wiki/Eligibility_for_the_NBA_draft "Eligibility for the NBA draft")

The draft was conducted under the eligibility rules established in the league's now-expired 2005 collective bargaining agreement (CBA) with its players union. The CBA that ended the [2011 lockout](/wiki/2011_NBA_lockout "2011 NBA lockout") instituted no immediate changes to the draft, but called for a committee of owners and players to discuss future changes.[[95]](#cite_note-2011_status_quo-134) The basic eligibility rules for the draft are listed below:[[96]](#cite_note-2011NBACBA-135)

* All drafted players must be at least 19 years old during the calendar year of the draft. In terms of dates, players eligible for the 2012 draft must be born on or before December 31, 1993.
* Any player who is not an "international player", as defined in the CBA, must be at least one year removed from the graduation of his high school class. The CBA defines "international players" as players who permanently resided outside the U.S. for three years prior to the draft, did not complete high school in the U.S., and have never enrolled at a U.S. college or university.
* The basic requirement for automatic eligibility for a U.S. player is the completion of his college eligibility. Players who meet the CBA definition of "international players" are automatically eligible if their 22nd birthday falls during or before the calendar year of the draft (i.e., born on or before December 31, 1990). U.S. players who were at least one year removed from their high school graduation and have played minor-league basketball with a team outside the NBA are also automatically eligible.
* A player who is not automatically eligible must declare his eligibility for the draft by notifying the NBA offices in writing no later than 60 days before the draft. For the 2012 draft, this date fell on April 29. Under NCAA rules, players will only have until April 10 to withdraw from the draft and maintain their college eligibility.[[96]](#cite_note-2011NBACBA-135)[[97]](#cite_note-NCAA_withdrawal-136)

A player who has hired an [agent](/wiki/Sports_agent "Sports agent") will forfeit his remaining college eligibility, regardless of whether he is drafted. Also, while the CBA allows a player to withdraw from the draft twice, the NCAA mandates that a player who has declared twice loses his college eligibility.[[97]](#cite_note-NCAA_withdrawal-136)

### Automatically eligible entrants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2012_NBA_draft&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Automatically eligible entrants")]

Players who do not meet the criteria for "international" players are automatically eligible if they meet any of the following criteria:[[96]](#cite_note-2011NBACBA-135)

* They have completed 4 years of their college eligibility.
* If they graduated from high school in the U.S., but did not enroll in a U.S. college or university, four years have passed since their high school class graduated.
* They have signed a contract with a professional basketball team outside the NBA, anywhere in the world, and have played under that contract.

Players who meet the criteria for "international" players are automatically eligible if they meet any of the following criteria:[[96]](#cite_note-2011NBACBA-135)

* They are least 22 years old during the calendar year of the draft. In terms of dates, players born on or before December 31, 1990, are automatically eligible for the 2012 draft.
* They have signed a contract with a professional basketball team outside the NBA *within the United States*, and have played under that contract.

22. **[^](#cite_ref-90)** On December 15, 2010, the [Los Angeles Lakers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Lakers "Los Angeles Lakers") acquired [Joe Smith](/wiki/Joe_Smith_(basketball) "Joe Smith (basketball)"), [Golden State Warriors](/wiki/Golden_State_Warriors "Golden State Warriors")' 2011 second-round draft pick and *[Chicago Bulls](/wiki/Chicago_Bulls "Chicago Bulls")' 2012 second-round draft pick (the 60th pick)* from the [New Jersey Nets](/wiki/New_Jersey_Nets "New Jersey Nets") (now [Brooklyn Nets](/wiki/Brooklyn_Nets "Brooklyn Nets")) in a three-team trade with the Nets and the [Houston Rockets](/wiki/Houston_Rockets "Houston Rockets").[[54]](#cite_note-87) Previously, on June 25, 2010, the Nets acquired *the second-round draft pick* from the [Milwaukee Bucks](/wiki/Milwaukee_Bucks "Milwaukee Bucks") in exchange for [Chris Douglas-Roberts](/wiki/Chris_Douglas-Roberts "Chris Douglas-Roberts").[[55]](#cite_note-88) Previously, on February 18, 2010, the Bucks acquired [John Salmons](/wiki/John_Salmons "John Salmons"), 2011 and *2012 second-round draft picks* and the option to swap 2010 first-round draft picks from the Bulls in exchange for [Hakim Warrick](/wiki/Hakim_Warrick "Hakim Warrick") and [Joe Alexander](/wiki/Joe_Alexander_(basketball) "Joe Alexander (basketball)").[[56]](#cite_note-89)

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-14)** Nationality indicates the player's national team or representative nationality. If a player has not competed at the international level, then the nationality indicates the national team which the player is eligible to represent according to [FIBA](/wiki/FIBA "FIBA") rules.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-23)** While Maurice Harkless was born and raised in the mainland United States, his maternal grandfather is [Puerto Rican](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico"). He has committed to represent [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Puerto Rico men's national basketball team") internationally since 2014.[[18]](#cite_note-22)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-39)** Ezeli acquired American citizenship in 2019.[[27]](#cite_note-38)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-44)** Taylor was born in Sweden to an American father.[[29]](#cite_note-42) He holds dual citizenship and plays for the [Sweden national basketball team](/wiki/Sweden_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Sweden men's national basketball team").[[30]](#cite_note-43)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-69)** Ognjen Kuzmić was born in [SR Bosnia-Herzegovina](/wiki/Socialist_Republic_of_Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Socialist Republic of Bosnia and Herzegovina"), [SFR Yugoslavia](/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia") (now [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina")), but has represented [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia_national_basketball_team "Serbia national basketball team") internationally since 2014.[[45]](#cite_note-68)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-86)** Robert Sacre was born in the United States to an [American father](/wiki/Greg_LaFleur "Greg LaFleur") and a Canadian mother. He has represented [Canada](/wiki/Canada_national_men%27s_basketball_team "Canada national men's basketball team") internationally since 2010.[[53]](#cite_note-85)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-102)** Laprovíttola also holds an Italian passport.[[67]](#cite_note-101)
8. **[^](#cite_ref-106)** Machado was born in the United States to Brazilian parents.[[69]](#cite_note-104) He holds dual citizenship and plays for the [Brazil national basketball team](/wiki/Brazil_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Brazil men's national basketball team").[[70]](#cite_note-105)
9. **[^](#cite_ref-110)** Mitchell acquired Libyan citizenship in 2023.[[73]](#cite_note-109)
10. **[^](#cite_ref-117)** Smith acquired Israeli citizenship in 2017.[[79]](#cite_note-116)
11. **[^](#cite_ref-123)** Wayns became a naturalized citizen of Belarus in 2016.[[83]](#cite_note-121) He played for the [Belarus national basketball team](/wiki/Belarus_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Belarus men's national basketball team").[[84]](#cite_note-122)
